question: In peptide-based self-assembly, what is the primary driving force for the formation of beta-sheet-rich nanofibers in aqueous solution?
options: Covalent disulfide bond formation between cysteine residues
Hydrophobic collapse of nonpolar side chains combined with intermolecular hydrogen bonding between peptide backbones
Van der Waals forces between aromatic side chains only
explanation: Peptide self-assembly into nanofibers is driven by a combination of hydrophobic interactions (excluding water from nonpolar surfaces) and backbone hydrogen bonding that stabilizes beta-sheet architectures. These non-covalent forces cooperate to form ordered supramolecular structures.
